aliases: Braised Chicken Feet in Abalone Sauce

english: Braised Chicken Feet in Abalone Sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for braised/deep-fried chicken feet with abalone sauce; mapped to broader chicken food product class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for chicken feet with abalone sauce; A00GJ (cooked chicken meat) is the closest defensible anchor. Chicken feet are a distinct anatomical part with higher collagen and lower muscle-meat content.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chicken feet with abalone sauce as a prepared dish.

serving.description: 1 plate (approximately 3–4 pieces of chicken feet with sauce)
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard dim sum serving of chicken feet with abalone sauce is approximately 150 g, comprising 3–4 whole chicken feet coated in sauce, as typically served on a dim sum trolley or ordered as a single dim sum basket.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, feet, cooked (SR Legacy #05358) and composite estimation using HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food database for sauce components
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked chicken feet (NDB #05358) provides the base protein, fat, and collagen profile. Sauce-component adjustments (sodium, sugar, carbohydrate uplift from abalone sauce, oyster sauce, soy sauce, fermented black beans, cornstarch) are estimated using HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food database entries for comparable Cantonese braised preparations. Input record values (energy 186.67 kcal, protein 12.0 g, fat 13.33 g, carbohydrate 4.67 g, sodium 566.67 mg per 100 g) are consistent with this composite and are retained. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Analogous estimate: braised meat dish with sauce components.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken feet with abalone sauce. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous braised meat dishes with sauce containing cornstarch and oyster sauce from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with low carbohydrate (4.67 g/100 g); the small cornstarch and sugar contribution from the sauce yields a low GI. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (35 × 4.67 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 2.5 g, confirming very low glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 566.67 mg per 100 g; a 150 g serving delivers approximately 850 mg sodium, representing 37% of the 2,300 mg daily limit in a single dish.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100 g, request reduced-sauce preparation, avoid adding soy sauce at the table, and account for this dish's sodium contribution within a daily total below 2,300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (4.67 g/100 g) and low GI (~35) present minimal glycaemic load, but deep-frying increases total fat (13.33 g/100 g) and caloric density, which may impair long-term insulin sensitivity.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes may consume a moderate portion (100–150 g) occasionally, prioritising steamed-only preparations where available to reduce fat intake, and pairing with high-fibre vegetables to moderate overall meal glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (566.67 mg/100 g), elevated total fat (13.33 g/100 g) including saturated fat (4.0 g/100 g), cholesterol (80 mg/100 g), and trans fat (0.07 g/100 g) from deep-frying collectively raise cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this dish as an occasional indulgence, limit serving to 100 g or fewer, choose steamed-only preparation to reduce fat and trans fat, and offset with low-sodium, high-fibre foods throughout the day.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; sodium at 566.67 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g RA threshold.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid the deep-fried preparation and request steamed-only chicken feet, reduce sauce quantity to lower sodium intake, and be aware that Shaoxing wine residue and fermented black beans are additional pro-inflammatory contributors.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Feet with Abalone Sauce (鲍汁凤爪, Bào Zhī Fèng Zhuǎ) is a classic Cantonese dim sum dish widely served in Singapore and across the Chinese diaspora. The preparation involves two distinct cooking stages: chicken feet are first deep-fried until the skin blisters and puffs, then soaked in cold water to achieve a spongy, gelatinous texture, before being braised or steamed in a rich sauce made from abalone sauce, oyster sauce, light and dark soy sauce, fermented black beans (douchi), garlic, ginger, Shaoxing rice wine, sugar, and cornstarch. The result is a deeply savoury, umami-laden dish with a soft, yielding texture from the collagen-rich skin and tendons, and a glossy, thick sauce that clings to each piece. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein (12 g/100 g) primarily from collagen and connective tissue, moderate fat (13.33 g/100 g) elevated by the deep-frying step, low carbohydrate (4.67 g/100 g), and high sodium (566.67 mg/100 g) from the multiple fermented and seasoned sauce components. The glycaemic index is estimated at 35 (low), yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 2.5 per standard 150 g serving. Clinically, the high sodium content is the primary concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management, while the fat profile warrants caution for cardiovascular patients. The dish is not suitable for halal diets due to Shaoxing wine and requires shellfish allergen disclosure for oyster sauce and abalone sauce. It is a culturally significant dish associated with Cantonese yum cha (dim sum) culture and is commonly ordered during family gatherings, weekend brunches, and festive occasions.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are consistent with USDA SR Legacy cooked chicken feet data and HPB Singapore database estimates for comparable Cantonese braised preparations, but no single approved source provides an exact match for chicken feet with abalone sauce as a composite dish. Values are retained from the input record as they align with the composite estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite Cantonese braised chicken feet dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or the HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food database as of 2024. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed trace/negligible): chicken feet with savoury braising sauce contains no meaningful vitamin C; value confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy chicken feet entry. trans_fat_g of 0.07 g/100 g is a low but non-zero value arising from the deep-frying step; retained from input record consistent with USDA data for deep-fried poultry products. Halal status is false due to the presence of Shaoxing rice wine (alcohol). Allergens include soy (soy sauce), shellfish derivatives (oyster sauce, abalone sauce), and gluten (soy sauce, Shaoxing wine). GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database.
